text:
PEMBROKE COURT FILE NO.:  928/95  
DATE:  2003/02/21 
 
 
ONTARIO 
 
SUPERIOR COURT OF JUSTICE 
 
 
B E T W E E N: 
)  
 
)  
MARLENE COLE and MAURICE COLE 
)
)
)
Henry J. Bradley, Q.C., and Ian R. Stauffer, 
for the Plaintiffs 
 
)  
Plaintiffs
)  
 
)  
- and - 
)  
 
)  
 
)  
THE CORPORATION OF THE TOWN OF 
DEEP RIVER 
)
)
)
Ben Marcus, Q.C., for the Defendant 
 
)  
Defendant
)
)
 
 
)  
 
)
)
HEARD:  November 18, 19, 20, 21 and 
December 9, 10 and 11, 2002  
 
 
REASONS FOR DECISION 
 
Power J. 
 
Overview: 
 
[1]      The Plaintiff, Marlene Cole, seeks an award of damages to compensate her for injuries 
she suffered on March 23, 1995, when she slipped and fell while walking on or near a concrete 
ramp that extended from the edge of a sidewalk to Ridge Road in the Town of Deep River.  
Maurice Cole, the husband of Marlene, seeks an award of damages pursuant to the provisions of 
the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F-3. 
[2]      The Plaintiffs allege that the Defendant failed to meet the statutory duty imposed on it to 
ensure that the sidewalk and/or ramp were free of sand or other debris and, as well, that the 
2003 CanLII 23452 (ON SC)
 
 
 
 
- 2 - 
 
 
 
Town was negligent in allowing the ramp to be constructed with a slope that was too steep 
especially when covered with sand.  In particular, the Plaintiffs allege that Mrs. Cole’s slip and 
fall on March 23, 1995 was “a result of sand accumulated from the winter season on the said 
ramp which caused it to be very slippery with the result that she suffered painful and serious 
injuries”. 
[3]      For the reasons that follow hereafter, this action must be dismissed. 
[4]      The parties are in agreement that the sidewalk and ramp are part of the highway or street 
and are, therefore, subject to the provisions of the Municipal Act R.S.O. 1990, c. M-45. 
[5]      The relevant section of the Municipal Act, s. 284 (1), provides as follows: 
 
284(1) Every highway and every bridge shall be kept in repair by the Corporation 
the council of which has jurisdiction over it or upon which the duty of repairing it 
is imposed by this Act and, in case of default, the Corporation, subject to the 
Negligence Act, is liable for any damages sustained by any person by reason of 
such default. 
 
[6]      The Act defines “highway” as follows: 
 
“Highway” means a common and public highway, and includes a street and a 
bridge forming part of a highway or on, over or across which a highway passes. 
 
“Sidewalks” are mentioned in subsections (4) and (6).  Subsection (4) provides that “except in 
case of gross negligence, a corporation is not liable for a personal injury caused by snow or ice 
upon a “sidewalk”.  Subsection (6) is a notice provision.  This is not a snow or ice case. 
[7]      I indicated to counsel that, given the definition of “highway” and the specific reference to 
“sidewalks” in subsection (4), I initially had a concern whether s. 284 applied to the sidewalk or 
ramp in question.  Counsel, however, were, and are, in agreement that s. 284 does apply.  Indeed, 
it has been judicially determined, apparently many times, that a “highway” includes sidewalks – 
(See, for instance, Larson v. Thunder Bay (City), [1999] O.J. No. 96 (Gen. Div.)).  In my 
opinion, a ramp such as the one here in question, is part of the sidewalk.  My reference to a 
“ramp” from time to time in these reasons should not be interpreted to mean that I am treating 
the sidewalk and the ramp as separate entities for legal purposes.   
[8]      The Defendant’s position is that the ramp was properly constructed having regard to its 
intended use in a high traffic area and, as well, it denies any failure to keep the ramp in a proper 
state of repair.  The Defendant further alleges that Mrs. Cole was entirely, or, in the alternative, 
substantially responsible for the slip and fall.  It submits, as well, that Mrs. Cole’s previous 
medical condition affected her ability to walk properly and was a contributing factor to her fall. 
2003 CanLII 23452 (ON SC)
 
 
 
 
- 3 - 
 
 
 
[9]      During the trial, both liability and damages were in issue.  With respect to the claim for 
damages, the Defendant alleges that Mrs. Cole suffered from a number of pre-existing physical 
and other problems predating the accident. 
The Parties: 
[10]      The Plaintiff, Mrs. Cole, is sixty-three (63) years of age – she was fifty-six (56) at the 
time of the accident.  The Plaintiffs have been married for forty-four (44) years and have three 
adult children and five grandchildren.  Mrs. Cole was not gainfully employed outside the home 
although she did, prior to the accident, conduct a part-time sewing business.  No loss of income 
claim was pursued at trial.  Mr. Cole is a retired teacher.  He is now sixty-seven (67) years of 
age.  He retired in 1990. 
[11]      Prior to the accident, Mrs. Cole babysat some of her grandchildren on a regular basis and 
actively played with all of her grandchildren – i.e. she would play ball, badminton and other 
games with them. This included playful chasing.  There is a dispute between the parties 
concerning just how active a lifestyle Mrs. Cole led prior to the accident.  She made a claim for 
loss of consortium. 
[12]      Prior to the accident, Mrs. Cole looked after the majority of the housekeeping chores.  
Mr. Cole suffered a heart attack when he was forty-eight (48) years old and has had heart surgery 
since then. 
[13]      The evidence substantiates that Mr. and Mrs. Cole enjoyed a good marital relationship 
before the accident and that, indeed, this good relationship continues.  Mr. Cole described his 
wife as being a person who kept busy, was always in good spirits, was active, was bubbly, and 
was generally in good health.  As aforesaid, however, there is an issue about the condition of her 
pre-accident health.  Indeed, the evidence shows that Mrs. Cole had suffered from depression, 
sometimes severe, for many years prior to the accident and the evidence also shows that she has 
encountered several physical problems over the years. 
[14]      It is clear from all of the evidence that she suffered numerous problems with respect to 
her shoulders, neck, back, legs and ankles.  Ms. Booth, her daughter, recalled that Mrs. Cole 
broke her ankle when pregnant with Ms. Booth’s sister in the 1960s and that Mrs. Cole “had a 
weak ankle – she’d go over on it”.  Mr. Cole, in his testimony, confirmed that she suffered from 
low back pain since the 1960s.  Considerable time was taken up during the trial with regard to 
Mrs. Cole’s medical history. 
[15]      Since the parties are generally in agreement with respect to the assessment of the general 
damages, I need not embark on a more detailed analysis of this evidence.  Mr. Marcus, counsel 
for the Defendant, however, submits that the medical history is also relevant to the question of 
liability, i.e. responsibility for the accident and/or contributory negligence. 
[16]      The Defendant, Deep River is a municipal corporation having a population of 
approximately four thousand (4000) people. 
2003 CanLII 23452 (ON SC)
 
 
 
 
- 4 - 
 
 
 
Additional Relevant Facts: 
[17]      The weather conditions on March 23, 1995 were wonderful for that time of the year.  
According to Mrs. Cole, it was an “absolutely beautiful” day.  Her evidence was that the sun was 
shining and that it was warm.  While there were some small pockets of snow in some areas of 
Deep River, there was no snow or ice in the area of the accident. 
[18]      Despite the fact that on the date of the fall there was no relevant winter snow 
accumulation, it is, nevertheless, not unusual to have snowfalls after March 23rd in Deep River. 
[19]      In the early morning of March 23rd, Mrs. Cole and her husband went for a walk of about 
forty-five (45) minutes duration.  Prior to proceeding alone to downtown Deep River on foot, 
Mrs. Cole ate her breakfast and showered.  Her intention was to atte
deciding_factor: The action was dismissed because the Plaintiffs failed to prove the Defendant failed to meet the statutory duty imposed by the Municipal Act and that the ramp was negligently designed.

text:
COURT FILE NO.:  00-BN-2565SR  
DATE:  April 28, 2003 
 
ONTARIO 
 
SUPERIOR COURT OF JUSTICE 
 
B E T W E E N: 
)  
 
)  
MARILYN MANNING and DAVID 
MANNING 
)
)
)
J. Sebastian Winny, for the Plaintiff 
Plaintiffs
)  
 
)  
- and - 
)  
 
)  
 
)  
3980 INVESTMENTS LTD.  
)
)
)
D. Bierstone, for the Defendant 
Defendant
)
)
 
 
) HEARD:  April 1-4, 7-9, 2003 
 
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT 
 
BELLEGHEM J. 
 
 
The Action 
[1]      The plaintiff sues for damages sustained when she slipped and fell on 
January 19, 1996 on premises occupied by the defendant. 
Issues 
[2]       
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 2 - 
 
 
 
1) Defendant’s liability; 
2) Plaintiff’s contributory negligence;  
3) Were the plaintiff’s long-term shoulder difficulties “caused” in the fall, or 
“by plaintiff’s employment”; 
4) General damages; 
5) Assessment, if any, of the plaintiff’s employment losses. 
The Evidence – The “Fall” 
[3]      The plaintiff is a 59 year old married woman.  She retired from a banking 
position after 22 years in 1994.  She took some time off and decided to return to 
work doing factory labour.  At the time of her fall, she was registered with 
Manpower doing odd jobs.   
[4]      She testified that on January 19, 1996 between 3:00 and 3:20 p.m., she 
had parked in the visitors’ parking at her son’s apartment at 2900 Battleford 
Road, Mississauga.  She was carrying a container of spaghetti in her hands while 
her purse was slung over her left shoulder.  She described it as a beautiful sunny 
day, mild in the morning but colder in the afternoon.  She was wearing a ski 
jacket, jeans and Nike runners.  Snow was piled up on the perimeter of the 
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 3 - 
 
 
 
parking lot which had not been salted or sanded.  She exited her car in the visitor 
parking lot and was proceeding along a 15 foot patio stone sidewalk to the east 
door of the building. 
[5]      The walkway was one patio stone width (18 to 24 inches).  Otherwise it 
was a flat open area.  There were no stairs or handrails along the walk. 
[6]      Visibility was excellent.  The walkway was all in shadow when she went 
into the building.  She was able to observe the condition of the walkway from the 
parking lot to the east door.  She did not see any ice or snow on the patio stones.  
Some of the stones, however, looked as though they had moisture on them as 
they were darker gray in spots.  The path was straight.  She could not tell the 
distance between the stones because they were too uneven.  Some of them 
were separated by one to one and-a-half inches and others were closer together.  
Some of the stones were up and some were down in height from the others.  She 
described the greatest height difference between the stones at about one inch, 
the depth of the stones themselves.  Most of the stones were uneven according 
to her testimony.  There was no salt or sand on the stones.   
[7]      She said that she stepped up off the curb, her right leg slipped and she 
went down on her right knee.  The place where she slipped, she said, was the 
second or third stone in from the curb.  She thought she was in the middle of the 
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 4 - 
 
 
 
third stone when she fell.  She said that as she went to take a step, her foot slid 
out from under her. 
[8]      At the time she fell, she said she was looking straight ahead to the door 
area.   
[9]      In cross-examination, she admitted that she had used the walkway on 
which she slipped quite frequently.  She said she noticed the stones were out of 
line.  She looked down as she stepped onto the curb.  She had seen details of 
the stones prior and also the day of the incident. 
[10]      She described the patch that she slipped on as being dark gray and 
looking like moisture.  It was not until she fell and she felt it with her hand that 
she realized it was soft ice, the type that forms in an ice cube tray before it 
becomes glassy.   
[11]      She agreed that she had made notes a couple of weeks after the 
incident.  She also agreed that she did not mention initially anything about the 
unevenness of the stones.  In her notes, she only refers to a moist patch and she 
agrees that she did not, in fact, see any ice until she fell. 
[12]      The plaintiff’s son, Brian Schram, testified that he had lived at the 16 floor 
apartment building for four years.  It was moderately maintained and housed 
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 5 - 
 
 
 
what he described as “lower income” residents.  The east door was used by 
residents who parked in the east lot. 
[13]      The pathway to the door was about 15 feet long, made up of two parallel 
lines of two foot square pavers.  They had been uneven for some time causing 
puddling and they were eventually smoothed out and leveled the following spring.  
The fence was put in later to keep people off the grass and was not there when 
she fell. 
[14]      When she fell, she said she hit her knee, her side, her hip and her elbow 
on the ground or the cement.  She landed on her hip and rolled onto her right 
elbow and arm.  She said that her whole arm contacted the cement walkway. 
[15]      She lay there a while and could see that her jeans were torn and that her 
knee was bleeding.  She thought she had the breath knocked out of her. 
[16]      She managed to get inside the east door and get to the superintendent’s 
office.  At the time, most of the pain was in her knee.  She went to the office to 
inform the superintendent of what had occurred and to ask why the walkway had 
not been sanded.  At the office, she met a lady who appears to have been the 
temporary superintendent’s wife who then called the temporary superintendent.  
She was unable to converse with him because he did not speak English. 
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 6 - 
 
 
 
[17]      The plaintiff testified that she had resided at the apartment building 
herself for a few months shortly before the incident and was familiar with a lady 
who did some of the cleaning.  She explained to this lady what had happened 
and complained to the people in the superintendent’s office that someone should 
immediately look after having the walkway salted or sanded.  She saw bags of 
salt against the wall by the door in the office. 
[18]      After lodging her complaint, she got to her son’s apartment. Her son, with 
the assistance of a lady friend, gave her some first aid.    He said that his mother 
limped into his apartment the day of the incident with blood on her knee saying 
that she had slipped on some ice by the side of the building on the sidewalk.  The 
east door was the closest entrance for her to come in from the parking lot.  He 
complained to the building staff later that evening that the path had still not been 
salted (about 5:00 p.m.).  He saw bags of salt on the floor of the maintenance 
office, including one that was opened and noticed that the front of the building 
had been salted but not the east side.   
[19]      Two and-a-half to three hours after her fall, her son escorted her to her 
car.  His mother showed him the ice patch where she had fallen.  He described it 
as a patch of ice two feet in diameter, eight feet from the curb of the parking lot, 
roughly in the middle of the sidewalk.   
2003 CanLII 2906 (ON SC)
DRAFT 
 
 
 
- 7 - 
 
 
 
[20]      She was able to drive home where she took some aspirins and went to 
bed about 7:00 p.m.  (Her son contradicted her on a couple of minor points.  He 
thought the motor vehicle accident she had been in was after her fall, and that 
she hadn’t actually lived at the building but had stayed over on occasion.)   
[21]      She contacted people at the building the next day and was told that a Mr. 
Horvat would call her.  She understood Mr. Horvat to be the property manager.  
She heard from him a short time later and told him how the fall had occurred.   
The “Expert Evidence” 
[22]      The plaintiff ca
deciding_factor: The defendant took steps to maintain the walkway, demonstrating an ongoing process of keeping the area safe.
